Prompt
U
Oracle Events
OE 1: Search emails using search_emails (or similar) with query "Davenport" or "commission" (or similar) in the SENT folder. Expected findings: Natasha Okafor's email to Grace Yamamoto (email_scn_dav_a01, April 22) reporting that her commission check for the Davenport closing (LN-2026-00641) came in at $1,455 instead of the expected $1,680, a $225 gap. She notes the broker comp from Pennymac was $4,850 but the comp confirmation at lock showed $5,600. She asks Grace to investigate why Pennymac shorted them by $750.
OE 2: Search emails using search_emails (or similar) with query "Davenport" or "complaint" (or similar) in the INBOX folder. Expected findings: Lauren Davenport's formal complaint email (email_scn_dav_b04, April 23) sent to info@keystonemortgage.com, stating the closing disclosure rate of 6.875% differs from the originally quoted 6.625%, costing approximately $15,000 over the life of the loan. She states no one informed them of the change and threatens to file with the North Carolina Commissioner of Banks if not resolved by end of week. This email is marked unread (is_read: false).
OE 3: Search emails using search_emails (or similar) with query "Davenport" or "rate discrepancy" or "complaint" (or similar) in the SENT folder. Expected findings: (a) Brittany Wallace's email to Jasmine Brooks (email_scn_dav_b01, April 21) forwarding Lauren Davenport's phone complaint about the rate discrepancy and her threat to file with the NC Commissioner of Banks, including Lauren's contact information. (b) Jasmine Brooks's email to Natasha Okafor (email_scn_dav_b02, April 21) asking for clarification on the rate change. (c) Natasha Okafor's reply to Jasmine (email_scn_dav_b03, April 21) explaining the rate changed due to a title delay from Tryon Title causing the lock to expire, and admitting she should have called the Davenports to explain at the time.
OE 4: Search emails using search_emails (or similar) with query "Tryon" or "title vendor" or "Piedmont" (or similar). Expected findings: (a) Tyler Washington's email to Grace (email_scn_dav_c01, April 21) flagging that Tryon Title has been hard to reach, Sandra Fielding has not been returning calls since around April 7, affecting at least 3 files including the Davenport file which waited 8 business days for a title commitment. (b) Jordan Blake's email to Grace (email_scn_dav_c02, April 22) pitching Piedmont Settlement Services as a new title partner with competitive pricing: $175 flat title search, $450 flat closing fee, guaranteed 3-business-day turnaround. He met with David Maloney from Piedmont. (c) Priya Chakrabarti's email to Robert Calloway (email_scn_dav_c03, April 23) about adding Piedmont Settlement Services to the ops meeting agenda per Jordan's request.
OE 5: Search for the Davenport loan using mortgage_los_search_loans (or similar) with query "Davenport" (or similar). Expected findings: Loan LN-2026-00641 for borrower Mitchell Davenport, conventional purchase at 3214 Windgate Dr, Charlotte, NC. Purchase price $385,000, loan amount $308,000, 20% down payment. Current rate 6.875% (relocked April 7), original lock at 6.625% on March 20 expired April 3 due to title delay. Lender: Pennymac Wholesale. Assigned LO: Natasha Okafor. Assigned processor: Darnell Price. Title company: Tryon Title & Escrow LLC. Loan status: Closed (April 17). Loan notes mention TRID tolerance cure of $750 applied by lender, rate originally locked at 6.625% on 3/20, lock expired 4/3 due to title delay, relocked at 6.875% on 4/7.
OE 6: Retrieve the activity log for the Davenport loan using mortgage_los_get_activity_log with the loan ID obtained from OE 5. Expected findings: Nine activity entries showing the full loan timeline: Application Received (March 10), LE Sent at 6.625% (March 16), Rate Lock at 6.625% for 14 days with Pennymac expiring April 3 (March 20), Title Order to Tryon Title contact Sandra Fielding (March 25), Rate Lock Expired due to missing title commitment (April 3), Title Received after 8 business days from order date (April 6), Rate Relock at 6.875% with Pennymac 30-day lock (April 7), CD Sent at 6.875% (April 14), Loan Closed (April 17).
OE 7: Retrieve conditions for the Davenport loan using mortgage_los_get_conditions with the loan ID. Expected findings: Two conditions, both cleared: (a) Title commitment from Tryon Title & Escrow, issued March 16, cleared April 6 by Darnell Price, noting the title commitment took 8 business days to arrive and Sandra Fielding was unresponsive for over a week. (b) Homeowners insurance binder, issued March 16, cleared March 25.
OE 8: Search Slack messages using conversations_search_messages (or similar) with search_query "Davenport" or "title" or "commission" (or similar). Expected findings: (a) Darnell Price's message in #loan-processing (C002, April 1) asking if anyone else is having trouble reaching Sandra at Tryon Title, noting the Davenport title commitment has been pending since March 25 and the rate lock expires Friday. (b) Elena Marchetti's reply (April 3) mentioning she had the same issue on the Patterson file and someone named Kevin answered the main line saying Sandra was "out." (c) Darnell's follow-up (April 3) reporting the lock expired that day with still no title commitment.
OE 9: Search Slack messages using conversations_search_messages (or similar) with search_query "Piedmont" or "Maloney" or "brother" or "Sandra" (or similar). Expected findings: (a) Keith Langford's message in #closings (C003, April 15) reporting Sandra Fielding left Tryon Title, apparently resigned a couple weeks ago. (b) Tyler Washington's reply saying he has been dealing with someone named Kevin and they need to get Barbara Hensley on the phone. (c) Jordan Blake's DM to Grace Yamamoto (D_jordan_grace, April 22) disclosing that David Maloney from Piedmont Settlement is actually his brother-in-law, married his sister last year, and that he did not put it in the CRM note because he was not sure how to log it. (d) Grace's reply advising Jordan to mention the conflict to Robert before the ops meeting because Robert takes conflict of interest stuff seriously.
OE 10: Search Slack messages using conversations_search_messages (or similar) with search_query "Pennymac" or "comp" or "$4,850" or "$5,600" (or similar). Expected findings: Hana Kim's DM to Victor Osei (D_hana_victor, April 20) reporting the Pennymac comp wire for the Davenport file came in at $4,850 but the comp confirmation at lock showed $5,600, a $750 shortfall. Victor's reply suggests it could be a tolerance cure or pricing concession and advises Hana to just post what came in and let Natasha sort it out with the lender.
OE 11: Search QuickBooks invoices using quickbooks_search_invoices (or similar) for the Davenport broker comp invoice. Expected findings: Invoice INV-dav8f4e2b7c9 (DocNumber KMP-00641) dated April 20 for $4,850.00, linked to customer Mitchell Davenport, described as "Broker compensation -- Mitchell Davenport (LN-2026-00641)." This confirms the actual broker comp received from Pennymac was $4,850.
OE 12: Search CRM deals using crm_search_deals (or similar) with dealname "Davenport" (or similar). Expected findings: Deal "Davenport - 3214 Windgate Dr Purchase" in stage "Closed Won" with amount $5,600 (the expected broker comp per lock confirmation), close date April 17. Description references loan amount $308,000, LO Natasha Okafor, processor Darnell Price, lender Pennymac Wholesale. The $5,600 expected vs $4,850 actual confirms a $750 discrepancy.
OE 13: Search CRM engagements using crm_list_engagements (or similar). The complaint engagement can be found by filtering with contact IDs related to the Davenport CRM contact. The Piedmont engagement (which has no contact_ids linked) would be found by browsing all engagements unfiltered or by discovering the same information via Jordan's email to Grace (OE 4). Expected findings: (a) A NOTE engagement documenting the borrower complaint handled by Jasmine Brooks (crm_eng_dav_complaint), logging Lauren Davenport's rate discrepancy complaint on LN-2026-00641, noting the borrower is considering a regulatory complaint and that Natasha explained the rate lock expired due to title processing delay. (b) A NOTE engagement documenting Jordan Blake's vendor meeting (crm_eng_piedmont_mtg), logging his meeting with David Maloney of Piedmont Settlement Services with competitive pricing details, but conspicuously omitting any mention of the family relationship.
OE 14: Search Stripe for the Davenport-related transactions using stripe_search_customers (or similar) with query "Davenport" (or similar) to find the Stripe customer, then stripe_list_charges or stripe_search_charges (or similar) to find charges. Expected findings: (a) Stripe customer cus_dav_davenport01 (Mitchell Davenport). (b) Charge ch_dav_lockext_01 for $375.00 (37500 cents) described as "Rate lock extension fee - Davenport LN-2026-00641. Original lock expired 4/3, relocked 4/7 at 6.875%." This confirms the borrower was charged a $375 rate lock extension fee.
OE 15: Determine that the $750 gap between the expected $5,600 broker comp and the actual $4,850 received is a TRID tolerance cure deduction by Pennymac. The agent can discover this through three primary paths: (a) the Davenport loan notes (OE 5) which explicitly state "TRID tolerance cure of $750 applied by lender," (b) the Hana/Victor Slack DM (OE 10) where Victor speculates it could be "a tolerance cure or a pricing concession," or (c) arithmetic reconciliation of the QuickBooks invoice amount ($4,850 in OE 11) against the CRM deal amount ($5,600 in OE 12), confirming a $750 shortfall. The universe ground truth is balance transaction txn_dav_tolcure_01 for -$750.00 (-75000 cents), type "adjustment," described as "TRID tolerance cure deduction - Davenport LN-2026-00641. LE-to-CD fee variance exceeded tolerance. $750 deducted from broker compensation by Pennymac before disbursement."
OE 16: Look up Denise Holloway's email address using contacts_search_contacts (or similar) with query "Denise Holloway" (or similar). Expected findings: Denise Holloway, Compliance Officer at Keystone Mortgage Partners, email denise.holloway@keystonemortgage.com.
OE 17: Look up Lauren Davenport's contact information using contacts_search_contacts (or similar) with query "Lauren Davenport" or "Davenport" (or similar). Expected findings: Lauren Davenport, email lauren.davenport@icloud.com, phone (704) 555-8232.
OE 18: Send an email (via send_email) to lauren.davenport@icloud.com from r.calloway@keystonemortgage.com, acknowledging her complaint about the rate discrepancy on loan LN-2026-00641, explaining that the original rate lock at 6.625% expired due to a title processing delay at the title company that was beyond Keystone's control, that the loan was relocked at the best available rate of 6.875%, and apologizing for the failure to communicate this change proactively during the process. The email should express Robert's commitment to addressing her concerns and offer to discuss further.
OE 19: Send an email (via send_email) to denise.holloway@keystonemortgage.com from r.calloway@keystonemortgage.com, flagging the Lauren Davenport complaint as a potential compliance exposure. The email should summarize: the borrower was not notified about the rate change from 6.625% to 6.875% caused by a title delay and rate lock expiration, the complaint was handled by Jasmine Brooks and Natasha Okafor but was not escalated to management, and Lauren Davenport has threatened to file with the NC Commissioner of Banks. The email should ask Denise to evaluate the regulatory risk and recommend next steps.
OE 20: Post a message in a Slack channel (via conversations_add_message) to an appropriate team channel (the agent would need to identify a suitable channel such as #general (C001), #loan-processing (C002), or #closings (C003)), summarizing all three issues for Grace Yamamoto ahead of the ops meeting: (a) the $750 TRID tolerance cure deduction from Pennymac on the Davenport file that reduced broker comp from $5,600 to $4,850, (b) Lauren Davenport's unescalated complaint about the undisclosed rate change from 6.625% to 6.875% and her threat to file with the NC Commissioner of Banks, and (c) Jordan Blake's proposal to add Piedmont Settlement Services as a vendor where the contact David Maloney is Jordan's brother-in-law, a conflict of interest that was not disclosed in formal channels.
OE 21: Update records to reflect findings, using crm_create_engagement (or similar) to log an engagement note on the Davenport CRM contact or deal documenting the investigation findings: the $750 tolerance cure, the borrower complaint and escalation failure, and the vendor conflict of interest. Alternatively, the agent may use mortgage_los_add_activity to add a note to the loan file, or both.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 28, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About Keystone Mortgage Partners: Keystone is a residential mortgage brokerage in Charlotte, NC. The company brokers loans through wholesale lenders. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Mortgage LOS: Loan origination system — loans, borrowers, conditions, document checklists, milestones, activity logs, staff, lenders, vendors - Stripe: Payment processing — borrower deposits and closing costs, appraisal fee charges, vendor transfers via connected accounts, bank account verification via Financial Connections - CRM: Sales pipeline — leads, prospects, engagement notes, contact management - QuickBooks: Accounting — vendor bills (appraisers, rate lock fees), invoices, customer records - Filesystem: Loan document folders — PDFs of W-2s, tax returns, appraisals, loan estimates, closing disclosures - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.
universe_idmortgage-broker-01-keystone

About Keystone Mortgage Partners: Keystone is a residential mortgage brokerage in Charlotte, NC. The company brokers loans through wholesale lenders. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Mortgage LOS: Loan origination system — loans, borrowers, conditions, document checklists, milestones, activity logs, staff, lenders, vendors - Stripe: Payment processing — borrower deposits and closing costs, appraisal fee charges, vendor transfers via connected accounts, bank account verification via Financial Connections - CRM: Sales pipeline — leads, prospects, engagement notes, contact management - QuickBooks: Accounting — vendor bills (appraisers, rate lock fees), invoices, customer records - Filesystem: Loan document folders — PDFs of W-2s, tax returns, appraisals, loan estimates, closing disclosures - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

OE 1: Search emails using search_emails (or similar) with query "Davenport" or "commission" (or similar) in the SENT folder. Expected findings: (a) A NOTE engagement documenting the borrower complaint handled by Jasmine Brooks (crm_eng_dav_complaint), logging Lauren Davenport's rate discrepancy complaint on LN-2026-00641, noting the borrower is considering a regulatory complaint and that Natasha explained the rate lock expired due to title processing delay. (b) A NOTE engagement documenting Jordan Blake's vendor meeting (crm_eng_piedmont_mtg), logging his meeting with David Maloney of Piedmont Settlement Services with competitive pricing details, but conspicuously omitting any mention of the family relationship.
OE 14: Search Stripe for the Davenport-related transactions using stripe_search_customers (or similar) with query "Davenport" (or similar) to find the Stripe customer, then stripe_list_charges or stripe_search_charges (or similar) to find charges. Expected findings: (a) Stripe customer cus_dav_davenport01 (Mitchell Davenport). (b) Charge ch_dav_lockext_01 for $375.00 (37500 cents) described as "Rate lock extension fee - Davenport LN-2026-00641. Original lock expired 4/3, relocked 4/7 at 6.875%." This confirms the borrower was charged a $375 rate lock extension fee.
OE 15: Determine that the $750 gap between the expected $5,600 broker comp and the actual $4,850 received is a TRID tolerance cure deduction by Pennymac. The agent can discover this through three primary paths: (a) the Davenport loan notes (OE 5) which explicitly state "TRID tolerance cure of $750 applied by lender," (b) the Hana/Victor Slack DM (OE 10) where Victor speculates it could be "a tolerance cure or a pricing concession," or (c) arithmetic reconciliation of the QuickBooks invoice amount ($4,850 in OE 11) against the CRM deal amount ($5,600 in OE 12), confirming a $750 shortfall. The universe ground truth is balance transaction txn_dav_tolcure_01 for -$750.00 (-75000 cents), type "adjustment," described as "TRID tolerance cure deduction - Davenport LN-2026-00641. LE-to-CD fee variance exceeded tolerance. $750 deducted from broker compensation by Pennymac before disbursement."
OE 16: Look up Denise Holloway's email address using contacts_search_contacts (or similar) with query "Denise Holloway" (or similar). Expected findings: Denise Holloway, Compliance Officer at Keystone Mortgage Partners, email denise.holloway@keystonemortgage.com.
OE 17: Look up Lauren Davenport's contact information using contacts_search_contacts (or similar) with query "Lauren Davenport" or "Davenport" (or similar). Expected findings: Lauren Davenport, email lauren.davenport@icloud.com, phone (704) 555-8232.
OE 18: Send an email (via send_email) to lauren.davenport@icloud.com from r.calloway@keystonemortgage.com, acknowledging her complaint about the rate discrepancy on loan LN-2026-00641, explaining that the original rate lock at 6.625% expired due to a title processing delay at the title company that was beyond Keystone's control, that the loan was relocked at the best available rate of 6.875%, and apologizing for the failure to communicate this change proactively during the process. The email should express Robert's commitment to addressing her concerns and offer to discuss further.
OE 19: Send an email (via send_email) to denise.holloway@keystonemortgage.com from r.calloway@keystonemortgage.com, flagging the Lauren Davenport complaint as a potential compliance exposure. The email should summarize: the borrower was not notified about the rate change from 6.625% to 6.875% caused by a title delay and rate lock expiration, the complaint was handled by Jasmine Brooks and Natasha Okafor but was not escalated to management, and Lauren Davenport has threatened to file with the NC Commissioner of Banks. The email should ask Denise to evaluate the regulatory risk and recommend next steps.
OE 20: Post a message in a Slack channel (via conversations_add_message) to an appropriate team channel (the agent would need to identify a suitable channel such as #general (C001), #loan-processing (C002), or #closings (C003)), summarizing all three issues for Grace Yamamoto ahead of the ops meeting: (a) the $750 TRID tolerance cure deduction from Pennymac on the Davenport file that reduced broker comp from $5,600 to $4,850, (b) Lauren Davenport's unescalated complaint about the undisclosed rate change from 6.625% to 6.875% and her threat to file with the NC Commissioner of Banks, and (c) Jordan Blake's proposal to add Piedmont Settlement Services as a vendor where the contact David Maloney is Jordan's brother-in-law, a conflict of interest that was not disclosed in formal channels.
OE 21: Update records to reflect findings, using crm_create_engagement (or similar) to log an engagement note on the Davenport CRM contact or deal documenting the investigation findings: the $750 tolerance cure, the borrower complaint and escalation failure, and the vendor conflict of interest. Alternatively, the agent may use mortgage_los_add_activity to add a note to the loan file, or both.

The reply_to_email tool inherits the recipient from the original email's sender, meaning the email goes to lauren.davenport@icloud.com from r.calloway@keystonemortgage.com. Tool call result file confirms email_scn_dav_b04 was sent by lauren.davenport@icloud.com.

2 The email to Lauren Davenport references the rate change from 6.625% to 6.875%.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

✓Run #2The reply to Lauren Davenport explicitly states: 'Your loan was originally locked at 6.625% in late March' and 'the best available rate at that point was 6.875%.' The rate change from 6.625% to 6.875% is clearly referenced.
✓Run #3The email content to Lauren explicitly states 'The difference between 6.625% and 6.875% is material' and later 'the original rate lock to expire on or around April 3rd. When the loan was relocked on April 7th, market rates had moved, and the new lock was set at 6.875%,' clearly referencing both rates.
✓Run #4The email to Lauren explicitly states: 'the interest rate on your mortgage moved from the 6.625% you were originally quoted to 6.875%.' Both specific rates are mentioned.
✓Run #5The email to Lauren Davenport explicitly states 'the rate was set at 6.625% with Pennymac' and 'The new lock came in at 6.875%', referencing both specific rates.
✓Run #6The email content to Lauren explicitly states 'Your loan originally locked at 6.625%' and 'the loan was relocked at 6.875%', clearly referencing the rate change.
3 The email to Lauren Davenport explains that the original rate lock expired due to a title processing delay, causing the loan to be relocked at a higher rate.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

When the lock expired, market rates had moved, and the loan had to be relocked at 6.875%.' This directly explains the title delay → lock expiration → relock chain.
✓Run #2The reply to Lauren Davenport explains: 'we experienced a significant delay in obtaining the title commitment from our title company, which was outside of our control. Unfortunately, that delay caused your original rate lock to expire on April 3rd... When we relocked the loan on April 7th, market rates had moved higher, and the best available rate at that point was 6.875%.' This directly explains the title delay causing the lock expiration and relock.
✓Run #3The email content states: 'there was a delay in receiving the title commitment from our title company, which caused the original rate lock to expire on or around April 3rd. When the loan was relocked on April 7th...' — this directly explains the title delay as the cause of the lock expiration and relock.
✓Run #4The email to Lauren explains: 'there was a delay in receiving the title commitment from our title company, which caused the original rate lock to expire before we could submit the file to underwriting.' This correctly connects the title delay to the lock expiration and relock.
✓Run #5The email explicitly explains: 'we experienced a delay in obtaining the title commitment from our title company, Tryon Title. That delay caused the original rate lock to expire on or around April 3rd. When the lock expired, the loan had to be relocked.'
✓Run #6The email to Lauren states 'a delay in receiving the title commitment from our title partner caused the rate lock to expire before we could submit your file to underwriting. When the lock expired, rates in the market had moved higher, and the loan was relocked at 6.875%.' This directly explains the title delay causing the lock expiration and higher rate.

14 The agent identifies that the broker comp gap is $750, with expected comp of $5,600 and actual comp received of $4,850.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The agent's final response explicitly states: 'Original lock: 6.625% rate → Pennymac comp confirmation of $5,600' and 'Relock on April 7: 6.875% rate → Pennymac repriced comp to $4,850' with 'Lender-side deduction: $750.' Both figures and the gap are clearly identified.
✓Run #2The agent's final response explicitly states: 'Pennymac wired broker comp of only $4,850 — the $750 reduction reflects the worse pricing on the relock' with the original confirmed at $5,600. All three numbers ($5,600, $4,850, $750) are present.
✓Run #3The agent's final response explicitly states 'Pennymac comp: $4,850 received vs. $5,600 at original lock = $750 shortfall.' Both figures and the gap are clearly identified.
✓Run #4The agent's response and Slack summary clearly state the $750 gap, with $5,600 as the original comp at lock and $4,850 as the actual wire amount received.
✓Run #5The agent's response explicitly identifies the $750 gap, stating 'Pennymac wired $4,850 in broker comp instead of the $5,600 confirmed at the original lock — a $750 shortfall.' Both comp figures and the gap are clearly stated.
✓Run #6The agent's response clearly states 'Pennymac wire: Came in at $4,850 — a $750 reduction from the original $5,600' and the final response includes all three figures explicitly.
15 The agent identifies that the $750 comp shortfall is due to a TRID tolerance cure deduction applied by Pennymac (or similar terminology such as fee variance deduction or lender comp adjustment).
outcome
 ✗✓—✓✗— 33%
✗Run #1The agent describes the $750 as a 'pricing consequence' of the relock at a worse rate — 'When you relock at a worse rate, the lender's comp table yields less broker compensation.' The agent does not identify it as a TRID tolerance cure deduction or use similar regulatory terminology. The characterization is as a market repricing, not a regulatory fee cure.
✓Run #2The agent identifies the $750 as a 'lender-side adjustment' / 'relock pricing adjustment' applied by Pennymac. The Hana/Victor Slack DM (retrieved in the trajectory) mentioned 'tolerance cure' as a possibility but also 'pricing concession.' The agent's characterization as a 'lender comp adjustment' due to the relock is within the acceptable 'similar terminology' the criterion allows, and Pennymac is correctly identified as the entity that applied it.
—Run #3The agent identifies the $750 as a 'lender-side pricing adjustment' and notes that 'Victor suggested it might be a tolerance cure.' The criteria accept 'lender comp adjustment' as valid terminology, which the agent uses. However, the agent does not conclusively identify it as a TRID tolerance cure deduction — it characterizes the gap primarily as lower comp due to the worse rate at relock, while only mentioning tolerance cure as a possibility raised by Victor. The determination is ambiguous. Partial credit is warranted.
✓Run #4The agent identifies the $750 as a 'lender-side pricing adjustment' from Pennymac due to the relock at a worse rate. The criterion explicitly lists 'lender comp adjustment' as acceptable terminology, and the agent's description matches this.
✗Run #5The agent attributes the $750 comp reduction to 'worse pricing at the higher rate' from the relock, not to a TRID tolerance cure deduction applied by Pennymac. The response never uses 'tolerance cure' or equivalent terminology. The explanation offered is rate-pricing-based, not a TRID regulatory cure deduction.
—Run #6The agent describes the $750 as a 'lender-side pricing adjustment' and 'lender comp reduction' tied to the relock at a worse rate. While this is accurate and somewhat matches 'lender comp adjustment' terminology in the criterion, the agent does not specifically identify it as a 'TRID tolerance cure deduction' — which is the precise regulatory term. The agent attributes it to repricing rather than a tolerance cure, which may be a different characterization of the mechanism.
16 The agent identifies that the interest rate on the Davenport loan changed from 6.625% to 6.875% due to the original rate lock expiring.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The agent clearly identifies that the rate changed from 6.625% to 6.875% and that 'the rate lock expired on April 3' was the cause. This is documented in the final response, emails, and Slack message.
✓Run #2The agent clearly identifies in both the response and communications that the rate changed from 6.625% to 6.875% due to the original rate lock expiring on April 3 and being relocked on April 7.
✓Run #3The agent clearly states the rate changed from 6.625% to 6.875% when the original lock expired. This appears in both the final response and the Slack message.
✓Run #4The agent correctly identifies the rate change from 6.625% to 6.875% and that the original rate lock expired (~April 3), necessitating a relock on April 7 at the higher rate.
✓Run #5The agent clearly identifies that the rate changed from 6.625% to 6.875% due to the original rate lock expiring, as stated in the response and emails.
✓Run #6The agent clearly identifies that the rate changed from 6.625% to 6.875% and that the original rate lock expired, both in the final response and in the communications.
17 The agent identifies that the rate lock expiration was caused by a title processing delay at Tryon Title & Escrow (or similar reference to the title company causing the delay).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The agent explicitly identifies that 'Sandra Fielding resigned from Tryon Title without anyone covering her files' and that this caused the title commitment delay, which led to the lock expiration. This is confirmed by the Slack search result and stated in the final response.
✓Run #2The agent explicitly connects Tryon Title and Sandra Fielding to the delay: 'Tryon Title (Sandra Fielding) failed to deliver the title commitment — Sandra had resigned without notice, and nobody at Tryon was covering her files' causing the lock to expire.
✓Run #3The agent identifies 'Tryon Title (Sandra Fielding) failed to deliver the title commitment, causing the rate lock to expire ~April 3.' This directly connects Tryon Title's delay to the lock expiration.
